description: The figure presents a weak-beam dark-field (WBDF) transmission electron micrograph showing a dissociated dislocation in the base austenitic steel alloy. The main micrograph captures two parallel partial dislocations separated by a stacking fault, with a scale bar of 50 nm. An inset selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern in the upper-right corner confirms the two-beam diffraction condition used for imaging. On-image annotations define the critical measurement parameters: d denotes the partial dislocation separation distance, α represents the angle between the dislocation line vector and the Burgers vector b of the undissociated dislocation, and g//b indicates that the diffraction vector g is parallel to the Burgers vector under the two-beam condition. The sample is the base Fe-Ni-Cr austenitic alloy subjected to 5% true strain compression to introduce dislocations. This image exemplifies the methodology for stacking fault energy (SFE) determination, where paired (d, α) measurements are input into the partial dislocation separation model. The figure supports the finding that the base alloy has an uncorrected SFE of 26.3 ± 3.0 mJ m⁻².

This figure presents a scatter plot showing the dependence of the austenite lattice parameter on silicon and aluminum alloying content in an austenitic steel system. The x-axis displays relative alloying additions in weight percent (0 to ~3 wt pct), while the y-axis shows the austenite lattice parameter in nanometers (0.358 to 0.360 nm). Three distinct data points are plotted: the base alloy (0 wt pct addition) with a lattice parameter of approximately 0.3585 nm; the 2.5 wt pct Al alloy, which exhibits a markedly increased lattice parameter of approximately 0.3598 nm; and the 2.5 wt pct Si alloy, showing a minimal increase to approximately 0.3587 nm. The visual comparison demonstrates that aluminum alloying significantly expands the austenite lattice, whereas silicon has a comparatively negligible effect on the lattice parameter. These measured lattice parameter values, obtained via X-ray diffraction, provide the critical structural input for subsequent stacking fault energy calculations and support the conclusion that Al and Si additions produce qualitatively different effects on the austenite crystal structure despite both being added at 2.5 wt pct levels.

Figure 3 presents two plots quantifying stacking fault energy (SFE) in austenitic steels via weak-beam dark-field TEM measurements. Panel (a) shows partial dislocation separation (nm) as a function of perfect dislocation character angle (deg.) for the base alloy, 2.5 Si, and 2.5 Al alloys (wt.%). Symbols denote measured data, solid lines represent calculated trends based on the average SFE for each material, and dashed lines indicate upper and lower bounds derived from the calculated SFE standard deviations. The graph reports uncorrected SFE values of 26.3 ± 3.0 mJ/m² for the base alloy, 19.9 ± 2.0 mJ/m² for 2.5 Si, and 38.3 ± 6.1 mJ/m² for 2.5 Al. Panel (b) summarizes the dependence of SFE on relative Si and Al alloying, confirming that 2.5 wt.% Al raises SFE while 2.5 wt.% Si lowers it relative to the base alloy. Together, the panels provide direct evidence that Al and Si have opposing effects on the SFE of austenitic steel.
